title: Optimistic UI Patterns

questions.question_text: What is the main goal of an Optimistic UI update?
options: To provide instant feedback to the user before the server responds
To prevent hackers from changing state
questions.explanation: By updating the UI immediately, the app feels significantly faster and more responsive to the user.

options: A UI that is always happy
Updating the UI immediately as if an action succeeded, before the server confirms it
questions.explanation: This makes applications feel instantaneous to the user (e.g., liking a post on Instagram).

questions.question_text: What is the biggest challenge with Optimistic UI?
options: It is hard to write CSS for
Handling errors; if the server request fails, you must 'roll back' the UI state
questions.explanation: You must gracefully undo the change if the backend returns an error.

questions.question_text: What is 'Race Condition' in data fetching?
options: When multiple requests are made and the older one finishes after the newer one, overwriting the data
questions.explanation: This can lead to 'flickering' or incorrect data being displayed.

options: A placeholder version of the UI (usually gray boxes) shown while content is loading
questions.explanation: Skeletons feel better than spinners because they map to the final layout.

questions.question_text: How do you 'Rollback' an optimistic update in React?
options: Saving the 'previous state' before the change and restoring it in the .catch() block of the API call
Asking the user to click undo
Automatically refetching data from the server at set intervals (e.g., every 5 seconds)
questions.explanation: Useful for simple real-time updates without using WebSockets.

questions.question_text: What is 'SWR' or 'React Query' used for?
options: Managing server state (caching, refetching, optimistic updates) in React apps
questions.explanation: These libraries handle the complex logic of data synchronization for you.

options: Data that is shown on the UI but might be different from what's currently on the server
questions.explanation: Great apps show 'stale' data initially while fetching 'fresh' data in the background.

options: A special component that catches JavaScript errors anywhere in their child component tree
questions.explanation: Error boundaries prevent the whole app from crashing if one component fails.

questions.question_text: What is the difference between 'Data Persistence' and 'State Persistence'?
options: Data persistence is saving to the DB; state persistence is keeping UI state (filter, scroll pos) during session
questions.explanation: Users appreciate when an app 'remembers' their current tab or filter after a refresh.

questions.question_text: Which HTTP status code signifies a request was accepted but is still processing (useful for optimistic UI)?
questions.explanation: 202 is the 'Optimistic' response from a server.

questions.question_text: Why do we avoid 'Prop Drilling' for global data like 'Theming'?
options: It makes the code messy and components hard to reuse since every middleman must pass the prop

questions.question_text: What is 'Throttling' in the context of UI interactions?
options: Ensuring a function only runs once in a certain time period (e.g., clicking a 'Submit' button)
questions.explanation: Prevents accidental double-clicks or heavy re-renders during window resizing.

questions.question_text: What is the 'React DevTools Profiler' used for?
options: Monitoring component performance and identifying why a component re-rendered

questions.question_text: True or False: Most users prefer a slow app that is never wrong over a 'buggy-feeling' fast app.
questions.explanation: Users perceive 'fast' as 'good'. Optimistic UI provides that 'fast' feeling even on slow networks.
